attrs.alt: Three-tier risk-based CA policy model: block High sign-in risk, MFA for Medium, SSPR for High user risk

content.text: Risk-Based Policy Model

Risk-based CA policies form an automated incident response system within Entra ID. When Identity Protection detects a suspicious sign-in or account compromise, a matching CA policy automatically enforces a remediation path — blocking access outright, requiring step-up authentication, or forcing a password change. This happens without any manual SOC intervention.
Policy
Conditions
Grant Controls
When Risk Remediates
Block High Sign-in Risk
signInRiskLevels: high
block
Never auto-remediates — admin must dismiss
MFA for Medium Sign-in Risk
signInRiskLevels: medium
mfa (OR)
Auto-remediates after user completes MFA successfully
Password Change for High User Risk
userRiskLevels: high
mfa AND passwordChange
Auto-remediates after SSPR completes; admin can also dismiss
Require MFA Registration
users not registered; action: register security info
mfa (register prompt)
Not a risk policy — enables just-in-time registration

Self-remediation is the key design goal: a user affected by a medium sign-in risk policy completes MFA, the risk auto-remediates, and they proceed with no SOC ticket. Only high-risk sign-ins (block) and high user risk (forced password change) require human resolution.
Deployment Prerequisites
Before enabling risk-based policies, verify these prerequisites are in place. Missing any one of them can cause legitimate users to be locked out.
Entra ID P2 license assigned to all users in scope (P2 is required for risk-based CA conditions).
Self-Service Password Reset (SSPR) enabled and configured for all users — required by the passwordChange grant control.
MFA methods registered by users — required by the mfa grant control in medium sign-in risk and user risk policies.
Break-glass emergency access accounts exist and are excluded from all risk policies.
Diagnostic settings configured to stream SigninLogs to Log Analytics for impact monitoring.
Deploying the Three-Tier Policy Set

Deploy all policies in Report-Only mode first. Wait 48-72 hours to collect sign-in data, review the KQL impact assessment, then enable each policy individually — starting with the least disruptive (MFA for medium sign-in risk) and finishing with the most aggressive (block for high sign-in risk).
MFA Registration Policy

The risk-based policies only work if users have MFA methods registered. Deploy an MFA registration CA policy to prompt unregistered users to register during sign-in, before rolling out the risk-based enforcement policies.
Enabling Policies After Validation

# Enable a CA policy (switch from Report-Only to Enabled)
# Run this for each policy after validating the Report-Only impact

# Get the policy ID by name
$policy = Get-MgIdentityConditionalAccessPolicy -Filter \
    "displayName eq 'CA-IP-01: Block High Sign-in Risk'"

# Switch to enabled
Update-MgIdentityConditionalAccessPolicy -ConditionalAccessPolicyId $policy.Id \
    -State 'enabled'
Write-Host "Enabled: $($policy.DisplayName)"

# To disable a policy without deleting it (for incident response rollback):
Update-MgIdentityConditionalAccessPolicy -ConditionalAccessPolicyId $policy.Id \
    -State 'disabled'
Infrastructure-as-Code with Terraform
Declare all three risk policies in Terraform for version-controlled, peer-reviewed deployments. Use a variable for the break-glass group ID so it can be overridden per environment.
Report-Only Impact Assessment

Use KQL to evaluate the Report-Only phase before enabling any risk policy. This shows how many users would be affected and by which outcome (block, MFA challenge, etc.), so you can prepare helpdesk capacity and user communication.

// KQL: Report-Only impact assessment for risk-based CA policies
// Run after 48-72h in Report-Only mode to understand deployment impact

// How many users would be blocked or prompted by each policy
SigninLogs
| where TimeGenerated >= ago(7d)
| mv-expand ConditionalAccessPolicies
| where ConditionalAccessPolicies.displayName has 'CA-IP-'
| extend PolicyName = tostring(ConditionalAccessPolicies.displayName)
| extend WouldResult = tostring(ConditionalAccessPolicies.result)
| summarize AffectedUsers = dcount(UserPrincipalName), Events = count()
    by PolicyName, WouldResult
| order by PolicyName asc, AffectedUsers desc

// Users who would be blocked by High sign-in risk policy
SigninLogs
| where TimeGenerated >= ago(7d)
| mv-expand ConditionalAccessPolicies
| where ConditionalAccessPolicies.displayName == 'CA-IP-01: Block High Sign-in Risk'
| where ConditionalAccessPolicies.result == 'reportOnlyFailure'
| distinct UserPrincipalName, IPAddress, RiskLevelDuringSignIn, AppDisplayName
| order by UserPrincipalName asc
Operational Monitoring After Enablement

Once risk policies are live, establish a regular cadence of KQL queries to measure policy health: how many events are being blocked vs challenged vs allowed, how many users are auto-remediating, and how many are calling the helpdesk because they cannot satisfy the MFA or SSPR grant control.

# Ongoing operational metrics after risk policies are enabled

// KQL: weekly summary — blocked vs challenged vs allowed by risk policies
SigninLogs
| where TimeGenerated >= ago(7d)
| where RiskLevelDuringSignIn in ('high', 'medium') or RiskLevelAggregated in ('high', 'medium')
| mv-expand ConditionalAccessPolicies
| where ConditionalAccessPolicies.displayName has 'CA-IP-'
| extend PolicyName = tostring(ConditionalAccessPolicies.displayName)
| extend Result = tostring(ConditionalAccessPolicies.result)
| summarize Events = count(), Users = dcount(UserPrincipalName) by PolicyName, Result
| order by PolicyName asc

// KQL: helpdesk indicator — users who failed MFA on medium risk (need assistance)
SigninLogs
| where TimeGenerated >= ago(24h)
| where RiskLevelDuringSignIn == 'medium'
| where ResultType in ('500121', '50074')  // MFA failed or required but not satisfied
| summarize FailedAttempts = count() by UserPrincipalName, AppDisplayName
| order by FailedAttempts desc

// KQL: users whose risk policies auto-remediated (healthy flow)
SigninLogs
| where TimeGenerated >= ago(7d)
| where RiskLevelDuringSignIn in ('high', 'medium')
| where RiskState == 'remediated'
| where ResultType == 0
| summarize Count = count() by UserPrincipalName, RiskLevelDuringSignIn
| order by Count desc
Common Errors and Troubleshooting
Error / Symptom
Root Cause
Fix
User blocked but no risk in portal
Offline detection updated risk AFTER sign-in; user blocked by stale state
Dismiss the risk if investigation confirms false positive
SSPR not completing password change
User has no SSPR methods registered, or SSPR is scoped to a group the user is not in
Register SSPR methods; check SSPR scope settings in Entra ID
Break-glass account blocked
Break-glass user was not added to the exclusion group
Add account to exclusion group immediately; dismiss current risk
MFA policy fires on non-risky users
CA policy conditions set incorrectly — missing signInRiskLevels filter
Verify policy conditions include signInRiskLevels or userRiskLevels
High user risk not triggering password change
User does not have passwordChange in grant controls, OR SSPR is disabled
Verify grant controls AND that SSPR is enabled for the user
Guest users blocked by user risk policy
Guest cannot complete SSPR in resource tenant
Exclude guests from user risk policy; handle separately with block or remove access
Emergency Rollback

If a risk policy causes widespread unintended blocks (for example, after a new detection type fires broadly as a false positive), disable all CA-IP policies immediately to restore access, then investigate. Switching to Report-Only is less disruptive than full disable if only a subset of users are affected.
Summary
Three-tier risk-based CA policies cover the key risk scenarios: block High sign-in risk, require MFA for Medium sign-in risk, force SSPR for High user risk.
Self-remediation is automatic: MFA completion remediates medium sign-in risk; SSPR completion remediates high user risk.
Deploy in Report-Only mode first; review KQL impact data before enabling each policy.
Prerequisites — Entra ID P2, SSPR, registered MFA methods, and break-glass exclusions — must all be verified before enabling.
Terraform manages risk policies as code, ensuring consistent deployment across environments and auditable changes via pull requests.
The MFA registration policy ensures users register methods before risk enforcement policies can lock them out.
